results.title: When to Apply for Graduate School | Month-by-Month Timeline
results.author: Lauren Thomas
results.text:
When to Apply for Graduate School | Month-by-Month Timeline

# When to Apply for Graduate School | Month-by-Month Timeline

Once you’ve decided to apply for graduate school, you need to carefully plan out the application process, leaving yourself enough time to:

- Choose which schools you’ll apply to
- Gather transcripts and recommendation letters
- Write your personal statement or statement of purpose
- Take any standardized exams you might need

In general, you’ll need to start preparing your application at least 6 months in advance of the deadline. Most application deadlines are about 7–9 months before the program’s start date.

## Table of contents

1. When is the right time to apply for graduate school?
2. Month-by-month timeline for grad school applications
3. Other interesting articles
4. Frequently asked questions about applying to grad school

## When is the right time to apply for graduate school?

Some students apply to grad school straight from undergraduate degrees, but it’s also common to return to school later in life. If you’re not sure yet whether you’re ready to apply for graduate school, ask yourself these questions.

### Career and field

- Do you want to change your career? Many individuals attend graduate school to enter fields like nursing, physical therapy, medicine, business, marketing, communications, etc.
- Do you need a graduate degree to progress in your field? In some fields (like law, research, most of health care, and business), a graduate degree is usually necessary to progress. In others (like software engineering or data analytics), degrees are less important.
- How much work experience do you need before applying to graduate school? For instance, MBA programs usually expect you to have several years of work under your belt, whereas many people start medical school right out of undergrad or soon after.

### Personal considerations

- Are you at a place in your life where you can focus most of your attention on school?
- Are you prepared to move across the country or even to another country if needed? If not, you should only apply to graduate schools near you.
- Does your family situation permit you to go back to school?

### Money matters

- Do you have enough money saved up or a realistic plan to finance graduate school? If you plan to take out government loans, carefully consider how much you will have to pay back after graduation with your expected earnings.
- Can you leave or cut back on your job without drastically harming your financial health?
- Do the graduate programs you’re interested in allow you to work part-time? For instance, medicine rarely allows you to attend part-time. This may also be subject to visa restrictions if you’re planning to study outside your own country.

## Prevent plagiarism. Run a free check.

## Month-by-month timeline for grad school applications

You should generally start thinking about graduate school around 18 months before you plan to start. Most program deadlines are 7–9 months before the start date, so you’ll have 6–9 months to get all your materials together, ask for recommendation letters, and take any necessary exams.

Note that some graduate schools—notably medicine—follow a different timeline. Also, some fields, particularly law, use rolling deadlines, meaning the earlier you get in your applications, the better!

The timeline below represents the most typical one, with a December submission deadline. If your deadline is earlier or later, you should adjust your timeline to match.

- Decide which graduate programs to apply for.
- Sign up and begin studying for any standardized tests you need.

- Continue studying.
- Begin asking for recommendation letters.

- Take any standardized tests for the first time.
- Make a list of graduate programs to apply to.

- Follow up on recommendation letters.
- Look into potential sources of funding.

- Begin requesting transcripts from institutions you’ve attended in the past.
- Retake standardized exams if you weren’t happy with your score the first time.

- Begin writing a statement of purpose and personal statement if needed.

- Edit your personal statement and keep in touch with your recommenders.

- Retake any final exams you’re not happy with.

- Perfect your statement of purpose.

- Make sure you have all the necessary materials.
- Send final reminders to your recommenders.
- Finish your applications!

- If you’re an American intending to take out loans to pay for graduate school, complete the FAFSA.

- Hear back from graduate schools and go for visits.
- Plan finances and apply for loans if necessary.
- Decide which school you’ll be attending!

| Month | Key tasks |
| --- | --- |
| March |
| April |
| May |
| June |
| July |
| August |
| September |
| October |
| November |
| December |
| January |
| Late February–April |

### March

Decide which type of graduate program you’d like to apply for and start researching schools that fit your criteria. Discuss which programs you should be aiming for with your former professors or current supervisors.

Most programs provide statistics about the test scores, undergraduate grades, or work or research experience of the students they accept. Aim high, but be realistic about your chances. Make sure to choose some programs that are likely to accept you.

Sign up and begin studying for whichever standardized test you need. Different programs require different exams, so you should make sure to check the website of the program you intend to apply to.

- Most graduate school programs in the United States require the GRE.
- Tests verbal and math skills, as well as ability to write a well-argued and logical essay.
- Usually administered on a computer in a test center, showing the test taker his or her preliminary scores at the end of the session.

- Specialized exams testing students on their knowledge in one of six areas: biology, chemistry, physics, psychology, mathematics, and English literature.
- Many graduate programs that require a high level of math require their applicants to take one of these exams.

- Required for law school admissions in the US or Canada.
- Tests logical and verbal reasoning as well as reading comprehension.
- Administered digitally, usually in a test center with other students.

- Digitally-administered exam for business schools admissions in the US and Canada (although many now also accept the GRE).
- Tests verbal and math skills.
- Adapts to the test taker, showing harder questions as questions are answered successfully and easier ones if they are answered incorrectly.

- Exam of choice for medical school admissions.
- One of the longest standardized exams, at 7.5 hours.
- Tests chemistry, biology, and psychology knowledge, in addition to verbal reasoning skills.

| Exam | What does it involve? |
| --- | --- |
| GRE (Graduate Record Examinations) General |
| GRE Subject |
| LSAT (Law School Admissions Test) |
| GMAT (Graduate Management Admissions Test) |
| MCAT (Medical College Admissions Test) |

### April

Continue studying for standardized tests. Study books can often be checked out for free from your local library. Aim to do at least a little bit of studying every day—that way, it becomes a habit.

Begin the process of asking for recommendation letters.

### May

Take the necessary standardized test for the first time. You aren’t penalized for taking the test multiple times and can send your best score, so don’t panic if you don’t meet your target score on the first try. However, note that each attempt costs (a significant amount of) money, so don’t completely slack off!

Make a list of the specific programs you’re going to apply to. One tip: organize your information in a spreadsheet with required materials, application fees, links to the online application sign-in, recommenders for each program, and deadlines. This will aid you later in the process!

### June

Follow up on rec letters. Now is a good time to begin face-to-face meetings with potential recommenders. Update your resume so that you can send it to recommenders and they can write you a strong letter.

Decide if you need extra funding. To plan your finances, make a monthly budget with expected rent, food, transportation, prorated monthly tuition/fees, and any other potential costs. If the cost is more than what you have in savings or expected financial support (including loans), then you’ll need extra support.

If you need funding, look into potential options—many, such as the National Science Foundation’s Graduate Research Fellowship Program, require extra essays and have earlier deadlines.

### July

Now is a good time to begin requesting transcripts, if you haven’t already. For most graduate applications, you must ensure you’ve requested transcripts from every university you’ve attended, even if you only studied there for a semester or it is located in another country.

Retake any standardized exams if you weren’t happy with the scores the first time around.

### August

Begin writing your statement of purpose. A statement of purpose is a short essay that discusses your professional and academic interests and background.

You may also be required to write a personal statement, which should talk about your personal story and personal motivations for applying to graduate school. It may include your potential to bring an underrepresented perspective or add to the diversity of the program you’re applying to.

### September

Send off your statement of purpose and personal statement to recommenders to aid their recommendation letters and to receive feedback.

It’s also a good idea to have your statements checked by a friend, family member or professional editor, who can help make sure your writing flows clearly and catch any grammatical mistakes.

A statement of purpose should be understandable to any professional in your field, even if they don’t specialize in your sub-discipline. Most graduate programs rely on a committee of professors throughout the field to evaluate applications, so there’s no guarantee yours will be read by an expert in your particular interest.

### October

October is generally your last chance to retake any standardized exam whose scores you’re not happy with.

### November

Perfect your statement of purpose and personal statement. It’s a good idea to take a week-long break from your applications so that you can approach them again with a fresh eye.

### December

Many graduate applications are due this month. Remind your recommenders of the final deadlines, and finish up your application.

Ensure you have your perfected resume, transcripts, and final personal statement ready. Upload them—and don’t forget to pay the application fee if that’s required!

### January

If you’re American and think you might want to take out any amount of loans to fund your degree, you should fill out the Free Application for Federal Student Aid (FAFSA). Once completed, you’ll be eligible for a loan to fund up to 100% of the total cost of your degree, including both tuition and living costs.

### February to April

Most graduate school results will come back in this time period. Many graduate schools offer in-person visit days in March and April. Some will even pay for your transportation and hotel costs. These visits will allow you to ask questions to faculty members and current students.

When choosing a graduate program, make sure to pay attention to how well the program fits your interests as well as its prestige. You should also pay attention to placement or job outcomes after graduation.

If you’re in a research degree, your supervisor is vitally important to your potential success—carefully evaluate your potential options (but remember that some advisors could leave, so you shouldn’t choose a program for just one potential supervisor!)

Try to hang out with current students in an informal setting to ask them questions you might not otherwise be comfortable asking. Ask what the work expectations are like—do they get time off? Do they feel like they’re being treated fairly? This is especially important for doctoral programs, which are several years long.

Make sure to read the fine print of any funding that you might receive. Will you have to teach or work in a certain position for a certain number of years afterwards? Choose carefully!

## Other interesting articles

If you want to know more about college essays, academic writing, and AI tools, make sure to check out some of our other language articles with explanations, examples, and quizzes.

College essays

Academic writing

AI tools

## Frequently asked questions about applying to grad school

When should I begin my applications for graduate school?

A good starting point to aim for is about 18 months before you would start the program, or 6–9 months before the applications are due.

In the first few months of the process, research programs and study for any standardized exams you might need.

You can then begin writing your personal statements and statements of purpose, as well as contacting people to write your letters of recommendation. Ensure that you give recommenders plenty of time to complete their letters (ideally around 2–4 months).

When are graduate school application deadlines?

Most graduate school applications for American graduate programs are due in December or January for a September start.

Some types of programs, especially law school, are rolling applications, meaning that the earlier you apply, the earlier you’ll hear back. In this case, you should aim to apply as early as possible to maximize your chances.

Medical school follows a completely separate timeline with much earlier deadlines. If you’re applying for medical school, you should speak to advisors at your university for more information.

At what point in life should I apply for graduate school?

Some students apply to graduate school straight from undergrad, but it’s also common to go back to school later in life. The ideal time to do so depends on various financial, personal, and career considerations. Graduate school is a big commitment, so you should apply at a time when you can devote your full attention to it.

Your career path may also determine when you should apply. In some career fields, you can easily progress without a graduate degree, while in others—such as medicine, business, and law—it’s virtually impossible to move up the career ladder without a specific graduate degree.

# Masters in Computer Science USC Statement of Purpose 2021

- Channel: Lauren Valdez
- Length: 15:24
- Views: 41.4K
- Likes: 683
- Published: 2021-09-08T14:56:34-07:00
- Category: Education
- Keywords: masters in computer science, masters in computer science essay, masters in computer science statement of purpose, masters in computer science tips, masters in computer science advice, grad school statement of purpose, grad school essay tips, grad school application, grad school essay, grad school tips
- Language: en

## Description

Applying to grad school? I read a Statement of Purpose from a student admitted to USC's Masters in Computer Science with a focus on Artificial Intelligence. I go over what makes this a good essay and common mistakes I see when reading graduate school essays. 

Download my sample winning grad school essays & get feedback during office hours when you sign up for my newsletter:
http://essays.laurenvaldez.com/

Get all my winning Grad Application strategies in my new book - Beyond Acceptance: The Transformational Guide to Applying to Grad School.
https://bit.ly/BeyondAcceptanceBook

## Transcript

i'm going to read this essay of a student who got into usc's masters of computer science program with focus on artificial intelligence as i read it i'll pause and make comments on some of the things that work really well in this essay and the techniques that you can borrow in your own essays you can always come to my office hours to get help on your essays let's dive in when the world was still operational and social distancing was merely a metaphor i embarked on a journey that galvanized me towards my goal of engineering socially conscious solutions for problems historically driven by human intuition cognition and expertise using data science and artificial intelligence this journey is the source and foundation of my interest to continue my academic enrichment by pursuing a master's degree my trip with data science started in my sophomore year at blank university with a video i watched about an accent coach describing the nuances and cadence pitch and intonation of different accents around the world as the video played my mind wandered and thought about virtual assistants and their speech discerning abilities specifically i wondered what could explain call devi turning into call of duty when announced with my parents south indian accent this peaked my interest to leverage data science speech processing and machine learning to help identify accents that characterize speech venturing guesses soon turned into discovering and learning about data science and the application of machine learning and solving my problem i caught myself consciously and unconsciously devoting my undergraduate studies to the praxis of these concepts so what works really well in this essay is how specific this person is right up front about what their interests are in computer science we see this excellent example about voices and how we talk and how that plays into artificial intelligence and we know up front where this person's interests lie one of the biggest problems i see in reading essays especially for masters of computer science is people are not very specific in what their interests are they're like i want to work at google and i'm interested in everything and that's not going to get you into a graduate program you really need to be specific with where your interests lie and what you want to explore further in a master's program i decided to identify and differentiate between indian accents and north american accents and voice recordings i employed speech analysis tools to break down the differences between the accents and machine learning algorithms to model the differences i posited a modification to the fabric of the input to the machine learning algorithm which increased the accuracy of detection of the different accents by 5.5 percent under the guidance of blank professor i wrote my first technical paper and presented it at the international conference for artificial intelligence and data engineering in 2019 the proceedings were published in the springer lecture series advances in intelligent systems and computing the following semesters i bolstered the fundamentals i acquired over the summer by enrolling in several courses in the spectrum of data science deep learning and natural language processing and applied my learnings in in subsequent projects here again we see some great specifics we first heard here's what the interest is and then now we're hearing how do you apply and approach that work of your interest in this case working on the differences between accents here we see that they wrote a paper here we see that they worked on speech analysis tools and when they put that into the machine learning algorithm they had this outcome of being able to improve the accuracy by 5.5 percent so that's a win here where we see that and in showing examples of the work you've done you want to be able to point to wins 0.2 outcomes and then we see an even bigger one here that their work was presented in this paper and published here which is an awesome win so anytime you can point to an outcome of the work you've done you want to do that strongly because it that's evidence that you know your stuff you know what you're doing eager to explore the role of data science and analytics in the real world i interned at blank company for two months where i worked on operationalizing property and casualty insurance claim analytics which would reduce the time and effort that goes into managing claims the most significant expense to any insurance company i developed a predictive analytic solution to forecast the severity of an insurance claim made by a policyholder to aid in the determination of insurance premium for the subsequent renewal cycle the end-to-end pipeline involve demarcating the impactful attributes and developing a severity model by comparing different algorithms parameters and metrics i created a wireframe that the company would be able to adopt to accelerate decision making by not having to rely solely on individual experience and professional judgment here we see another example of their work and their interests that they have when it comes to an essay you know there's many projects that you've worked on you probably want to highlight you know three projects in detail that you've done that you've had strong outcomes for if you have more space to write like this is a two-page 1200 word essay then you can describe more projects but you don't necessarily need to describe every project you've ever worked on because a you'll have your resume to help with that and b you want to describe projects that are going to show your interest and your ability so pick the best projects you've worked on that are going to make the best case for you getting into this grad program continuing my journey to work on on problems reliant on domain knowledge and high human intervention i worked with professor blank for 11 months on cutting ed research in the field of bioinformatics and molecular informatics i worked on research that finds application in drug repositioning and the artificial synthesis of drugs which heavily rely on domain knowledge years of effort and millions of dollars the crux of the project involve determining the therapeutic class of a drug given information about its function gene expression or structure simplified molecular input line entry system i.e smiles a textual representation of the molecular structure during the first six months i worked on creating a triple network and architecture gaining popularity in the image processing sphere that models gene expression data the network performed well however due to resource and time constraints it could not be developed enough to surpass the performance of the current benchmark so i pivoted the following months i worked on creating architectures inspired by natural language processing to model the structure of the drug i found that previous research in this niche focused on predicting the chemical and physiological properties of known drugs with the advent of synthetic drug discovery a system which relied solely on a universal identifier like a drug structure would be a significant push for data-driven drug discovery and drug repositioning this was the precedent for blank project a suite of tools to aid in and serve as a benchmark for classifying drugs into overarching functional groups purely based on the chemical structure the networks of blank project predict the anatomical therapeutic chemical classification established by the world health organization with accuracies as high as 80 percent which is five times higher than the baseline classification my co-author and i presented blank project at the 2025th international conference on research in computational intelligence and communication networks and our work is set to be published in ieee explore this is another great example of this person's experience we see in this paragraph that they have a diversity of experience they really know what they're talking about and we see another win here with this example of this project which has an 80 accuracy and being able to present their work at this conference so you see here there's strong examples that show the caliber of this person's work one of the biggest problems i see in masters in computer science statements of purpose is not enough experience where you know people are like well i took these courses where i learned these programs but you're not actually describing any wins like what did you do with that knowledge how did you apply the use of those skills and then those techniques to develop something to produce an outcome that often tends to be missing and maybe you don't have enough experience yet to be applying to a master's in computer science and i'm showing you this example to see this is the level of experience people have going into applying so if you've only done coursework where you haven't been able to demonstrate your your skills and your abilities and having these kinds of wins then you may need to gain more experience in the field before you apply to a master's in computer science because everyone applying is going to have a lot of examples like this and you may need to bolster your experience first during my last semester at college i internet goldman sachs while simultaneously pursuing my research with professor blank throughout my year of research and corporate work experience i engage in an internal dialogue about the ways architectures can shift paradigms not only in their intended field of application but in seemingly unrelated spheres i watched how natural language processing powered a molecular informatics project how image processing lent itself to genomics how machine learning automated something as intuitive as discerning an accent how data analytics transformed a financial institution i hope to leverage this transcendent nature of computer science as a whole in my career as an engineer all while being mindful of the social ramifications of non-inclusive algorithms operating at a large scale in a world run by massive ai giants and startup bubbles the race to get the best product out blinds us to the ramifications of bias systems i want to engineer socially conscious inclusive solutions to problems that might seem commonplace like the non-comprehension of my accented speech by virtual assistants and those that are critical like the diagnosis of a pneumonia-like illness in historically disadvantaged minorities i want to continue working in data analytics and machine learning while expanding my portfolio to work on the social implications of scalable artificial intelligence to abate encoded social bias in today's ai algorithms this is my motivation to pursue a masters of science in computer science artificial intelligence at the university of southern california these two paragraphs right here are critically important because they show us who this person is and what this person cares about that they're interested in reducing unconscious bias that shows up in artificial intelligence and there's many examples of this in our technology you know like the racial profiling that happens in technology all that kind of stuff and this person saying hey this is a problem and i want to do something about it makes this person stand out to me like if i'm on admissions committee i'm thinking yes this is an awesome thing you want to pursue and i want to support you in pursuing that because i think that people who think that way should be leaders in this field and so this makes that person stand out what i would have done if i was this person um is i actually would have made this paragraph i would have made it the intro paragraph because it so clearly sets up what this person cares about and i think as an intro it would have been stronger than what they have you know what they have is fine but this would have made them stand out at the get-go even further because it's a really great paragraph that highlights what this person cares about um and when you are writing your essays you want to think about um what in the bigger broader sense of the world is the thing that you care about that you want to move forward in the world and it doesn't need to be something that is um you know changing social inequities but it could be something that is important to you and the kind of work that you want to do so think about what is it that you care about and how do you want to use technology to move forward the thing you care about and make that very clear and explicit in your essay because that shows who you are as a person and and that's going to make you stand out from the other candidates because not everybody cares about the same things you care about and that's a way you can kind of make yourself stand out from the pack the profundity of the curriculum and research conducted at the vertebee school of engineering is perfectly positioned to prepare me for a career in ai sensitive to social inclusivity and trust i am excited at the possibility to learn from and work with professors who are at the heart of innovation working on pioneering research i relate to the work on speech recognition specific to non-native speech and inclusivity at the signal analysis and interpretation laboratory as it aligns with my motivation and with earlier work in the field courses like speech recognition and processing for multimedia will solidify my foundation and allow me to improve my work in the field and take it to scale working with professor blank at usc and professors at the usc center for artificial intelligence and society on fair and interpretable machine learning would be a great conduit to my goal of creating socially conscious intelligence systems for problems driven by human expertise and sensitive to bias so this last paragraph very specifically ties what this person wants to do to the resources at usc and this is something that you need to make crystal clear in your essays what do you want to do and how is that university set up to make you successful in what you want to do and throughout all this person's essay we see them coming back to the same theme around uh language and looking at language and accents in artificial intelligence and then we see that usc has a whole center to do that they have classes specific to that they have professors who that's what they're working on and that makes usc a really great fit for this person um and i'm sure that's the reason you know why this person is going to that school and this is a big mistake i see in a lot of essays where people are not being clear on why that school is a good fit for what they want to do so you want to make sure you go through the curriculum you go through professors you go through the resources that they have at the university to understand what are the resources they have and how do those resources match up to what you want to do and to summarize what makes this a great essay is that it's very specific on what the person wants to do what they're interested in it highlights the diversity of their skill set and multiple examples of them being successful at applying what they know and being able to present at conferences and you know improve the accuracy in their machine learning all that stuff so has lots of great examples and it's really clear that the university is a really great fit for what they want to do so take those lessons into your essays be specific show examples that show the amazingness of the work that you've done and be very clear and specific in how the university is going to support you in being successful in the career you want to have if you want feedback on your essays you can come to my free monthly office hours or my private office hours and you can also download a pdf of some of my sample essays uh in the links below in # How to Apply to CS Masters in the US | Application Tips & Process

- Channel: SoojinTech
- Length: 19:36
- Views: 1.1K
- Likes: 36
- Published: 2025-11-08T09:30:03-08:00
- Category: People & Blogs
- Keywords: gradschool, masters, MSCS, UPenn Masters, UPenn MCIT, Masters in the US, Masters Application, Applying to Masters
- Language: en

## Description

Hi Everyone,

In this video, I share a step-by-step guide on everything you need to know to apply for Master’s programs in the United States, specifically for Computer Science graduate programs from UPenn MCIT, Georgia Tech MSCS, NYU MSCS and more!

It’s been a while since I applied to grad school, but I still remember how overwhelming the process felt at first — and how much I wished I had a friend or a sister who could guide me through it. 

So here it is! I’ve outlined the entire application journey — from preparing for the GRE, writing your Statement of Purpose, budgeting for application fees, choosing which schools to prioritize, and more — along with my personal tips and insights.

I hope this video is helpful and encouraging as you start your own application journey!

~Let's connect~
♡ 💌 slee06110@gmail.com
♡ Insta @soojin.jenny

#masters #upenn #mscs #mastersprogram #graduate #applicationtips #application #gradschool

## Transcript

Hi everyone, welcome and welcome back to Sudin shares and today we are going to talk about how to apply to masters in computer science in the United States.
Before we get started, a little background about myself. I'm studying computer science at University of Pennsylvania. After undergrad, you'd best believe that you'd never have to write personal essays ever in your life.
But if you are at a crossroad where I was a few years ago where you are staring at these applications and having to come up with personal essays and making [clears throat] applications to these schools. Here are some of things that you need to know. So everything starts with doing your research and shortlisting which schools that you would like to apply to. I made more detailed video on how to choose the best school for you. What are some of the considerations you might want to make.
So I will link that video right here. So please go check that video out and then we can come back to this video. So great. Now you have shortlisted schools that you want to apply to whether it's one or 10 schools. Although I'll recommend that you apply more than a couple more schools than just one. Then the next step is called plan plan. I use notion. Um you can use any type of productivity tool to keep track of the things that you need to prepare for that for the schools that you're applying to.
What are the deadlines? There might be some early deadlines and secondary deadlines and each school has different deadlines. So make sure you take notes of those on your notion or on your Google calendar. Also do research on how much do you need to pay for application, what do they require you to submit and so on. Um typically some of the things that you need to prepare for to apply for masters in computer science in the United States are statement of purpose or any other custom field question that a school asks. Uh recommendation letters different school asks different number of recommendation letters typically two or three uh from your professors or former employee employers. You would need to send your transcript so your GPA GRE. Yes. A lot of the schools do require you to take yet another standardized test. So I will get back to that later in this video. If you're international student or are not from strictly English-speaking countries, uh they will require you to take TOEFL perhaps portfolios uh as well as application fee. So these are some of the things that you need to prepare in advance. So um literally like create a table of all these above items and write down rows of schools that you want to apply to and start check marking whatever that you're ready and what you're uh still in need. This will really help you to track and visualize how far um you have prepared for um as well as to prioritize what you need to start doing. So once you have written down all the things that you need to start preparing start study for JRE and also find a date to take an exam because without a deadline on which day you're going to um take an exam write an exam I noticed that people tend to really dep prioritize studying GRE over busy academic schedule. So I highly highly recommend that you set a date and then just once you time box how much how many weeks that you have got you'll find yourself more motivated motivated to study. Uh so there's two sections for JRE one is verbal section and quant se section for CS my experience was that you just need to do very well on this quant section. So I got 170 out of 170 a full mark for quant section. So I was quite confident that my math or logic logical skills are proven but then my verbal score was not good. It was I think just around average I believe or just above or just around average.
Definitely not a stellar score that I would think that I could apply to some of the most competitive schools. But I was like, hey, I'm applying for CS masters program and my quant uh scores are good. So then I just literally took a chance and I for as far as my experience goes um I got into some of the um most competitive schools. So I would say um do well on quant um if you have limited time like just study a lot for quant section. Um, another thing that I would highlight is that if you can't, um, try to take GRE earlier than your immediate deadline for application.
So then if you mess up your first trial, you have enough time to uh take second JRE and then not only taking you would also have to wait for the results to come out and also for them to send the uh official score to school. It takes weeks. So then let's say your deadline is 1st of November and there's a GRE that you could take on um like 20th of October. You might think that you have taken the exam so you have enough time uh for application but in reality that's not the case because it will take some time for your results to come out and for them to send uh the results to school. So that leave enough time um at least a month I'll say before your first deadline. Um for my case I took twice because my first GRE exam didn't go very well. One thing to keep in mind is that that and what I have underestimated was that the exam is like freaking three or four hours long and I think I lost stamina in the middle of the exam. I got like so tired and I couldn't really like stare into monitor for that long with a lot of focus. Um so then I think whatever the second half was I did really poorly. So then next time when I am taking J at least I knew what I was getting myself into. So then I think it has become easier for me to manage my stamina I'll say. So yeah, try a mock [clears throat] exam just like how you in real exam setting because I think I've just taught it without a time pressure. So that's one thing that I would recommend you guys doing. Fourth is writing um personal essay or statement of purpose essays. Uh I'll say do this alongside JRE uh if you are not a quick writer because at least for me it takes a lot of time for me to come up with this statement of purpose related essays and you would want to take feedback from your professors, friends and then um it will obviously take time for you to do some research. So do do not leave it until the last minute.
That's what I want to say. Um, so the essay wasn't as vague or broad in comparison to applying for undergrad because I remember putting a lot of effort and thought into like undergrad college application to liberal arts school because um I didn't have a specific major or like career purpose or career goal in mind then. So then my essay would have to be a lot more uh personality based and like yeah so it was harder for me to find a focus and really try to uh show me as a person and why I am a good fit for the school.
However, masters are a lot more specialized and you must be applying to masters to achieve specific goals and mission, right? Like for undergrad, I feel like a lot of people just apply because we grow grow up in this society where like we need an undergrad degree.
But then for masters I feel like because of the cost uh and also just the time commitment um people tend to apply because they're genuinely interested in the things that they are going to learn or um at least they have very specific outcome what they're looking for. So I think then writing an essay becomes easier because um you have a strong purpose and mission that you can talk about. However, this doesn't mean that you have a shortcut to writing essay or skip doing research on school labs that you might be interested in, courses that you want to take, what specific effort that you have put in thus far that makes you a good candidate and so on. So, um it will take time for you to do such research. Some tips that I'll give is do like research uh on a lot of the for me because it is like computer science program but I can imagine how it can translate to other uh majors as well like re reach out to the research labs, professors, posttos or like student interest group or other related things that you can join. get an idea from there on what are the some of the very specific things that this school makes a great option for you and how you can be an addition to the community and for their research community. Um I think those are some of the most direct way to show among many other schools why this school is particularly for you. Yeah. So that's perhaps one of the most well-known tips but still I would like to emphasize. I guess number five what you need to prepare is getting recommendation letters. I feel like for all these bullet points I'm saying start early, start early. But then I guess it is really important because I really think that I thought I had started my preparation earlier but yet I just felt like uh I did not have enough time to do all this one specifically takes longer than you might think initially. So start asking early. uh let your potential professors know that you think that they might be able to give you a strong letters of recommendation for XYZ schools and ask them if they feel comfortable uh or feel like they know you well enough to give you or write you a good recommendation letter. I think a lot of the students make mistakes here where they would just reach out to one of the most famous professors uh in the field or just current professor who you're taking classes just because you have an easy access to them.
Those are valid approaches but I do feel like there's two different things. a very strong professor who's well known in the industry may not be the best person to write you a very strong recommendation letter. So I do think it's really important for you to like really reflect on your college classes and your relationship with professors and identify who might who you think would be a great person to really advocate for you. But then also make sure to ask them if they're willing to do that and if they feel comfortable doing so because I think writing a recommendation letter will be a quite a long journey. It doesn't end them just writing one paper and call it a day. For each school that you apply to, they will need to submit the application letter to the school portal. So there will be a lot of emails back and forth. So try to find reliable professor also um because you want to make sure that they submit those recommendation letters on time.
Another thing that I would highlight is that if the school's asking you to get recommendation letters from three professors, reach out to four professors. Um this is something that I learned through my experience where I have had three professors who initially were willing to give me recommendation letters but life happens and honestly like things can happen to professor as well. So during the process of application one of the professor was on zapical and he was no longer in touch.
So then this brings back to this point of like yeah it's rather a long journey and you will need their support um throughout this journey. So so yeah make sure you um ask around more professors than what is required just in case something like what happened to me happens to you. It is just unfortunate things can happen to professors as well, right? So that although they have the best intention to support you um if they're not in a good position to help you, they won't be able to help you on time. So um yeah, it's at your advantage to really up manage um this tasks and be conservative, I'll say, and like do more than what you think is necessary. Yeah.
Just um to highlight um again this process is the part where you would really need to upmanage um your professors because you're the one who needs that recommendations to come through the most and then they're busy with other works as well. So, make sure you track for each applications when is the deadline for recommendation letters to have come through. And really, just like how your professors are giving you an assignment in reverse, this time it's your turn to give them an assignment. Hey, I need your application recommendation letter to be submitted by this date. I'll remind you a few days later if I don't see Yeah. If I don't see that it has been submitted. Things like that. it you will do them a favor like it I struggled a little bit with this because I have built this rapport with my professors on where they are giving me tasks not me giving them tasks but it's I think absolutely necessary for you to step up and do that um for your own benefit I don't I lost track of numbers but I think it's number six uh is to prepare and take TOEFL I really put this until aside until the last minute because I was like hey I don't need to take TOEFL exam like this is literally English is the only language that I have gotten any higher education from and I graduated from American college and then I just thought that I would never have to take TOEFL but then when I did further research uh seems like unless you are from UK, US, Australia or a few other English speaking country they are asking you to submit TOEFL. Some of the schools do, not all of the schools, but at least some of the schools that I was going to apply to did require. So, I remember like trying to find a place to take TOEFL last minute. Don't be like me. Uh because it will be a bit stressful trying to book a TOEFL exam and u make sure they send your results on time. So, do some research and identify whether you have to take it or not. And if you have to take it, just go and take it literally. I think TOEFL exam if you are applying for masters in the US and if you can watch my video and follow along I think you're good. Um I didn't study at all. I just went and took an exam. I don't think you have to like get a stellar result for TOEFL. So just go take an exam just so that you have the requirements met. And seven, you another thing that you may not think proactively but that happens is that you need to pay to apply to these schools. So application fee is something that al you you also need to prepare for each school. I remember paying around $100.
It depends. Uh but I think it was around $100 per school. So if you're applying for 10 different schools, that's $1,000 US. And it does add up. But as a student that might be a significant amount of money so be prepared for that. There are some ways to get this application fee waved. So it might be also worthwhile taking some time to look into ways in which you could have this application fees waved. But just to know that it was around $100 US per school. Yeah. And also um taking JRE, taking TOEFL and having those scores officially submitted to each schools that you're applying to also costs money. So um it all adds up and yeah so I just wanted to say it up front so that it's less of a surprise for you guys as you are preparing to apply for masters. Uh yeah, so these are some of the things that you will need to apply to master's program in CS and it is a lot. I remember doing this alongside full-time recruitment as well as just being a full-time student trying to finish my thesis and trying to pass algorithm exams and things like that.
So, I know by firsthand how uh busy your life can be in your senior year and and that's why I hope um at least me walking through some of the things that you would need to prepare um are helpful and um one thing that I would also like to share is that it it wasn't really a strategic way to plan this way but it turned out well was that I have really focused earlier on in the semester on applying for masters. So then because most of my applications would have done by November. So I just spent most of my efforts in just getting the academics done and applying for masters and then once uh I have finished applying uh I geared myself up for full-time recruitment. So then there was at least at at least a division of focus instead of me just like trying to apply for everywhere from masters to companies and I think that would have been really stressful. So um maybe plan out maybe depending on your program um if your applications can be done earlier just really focus on getting those applications done and you'll have enough time for also recruit full-time. Yeah.
pre-plan and plan and plan and plan early and start early is one thing that I'll really emphasize. Yeah, I really wish that somebody sat me down and told me these are all of the things that you need to prepare in order for you to get into master's program. So, uh I hope you guys find this information super helpful and I hope you guys get a result that you guys had wished and let me know in the comment what are your some of your dream schools and programs you want to um get into or you're planning to apply to. And thank you so much for watching this videos and I will see you guys in the next video. Bye.

results.title: Crafting a Competitive Statement of Purpose and Letter of Reference Strategies
results.author: Ohio State
results.text:
# Crafting a Competitive Statement of Purpose and Letter of Reference Strategies

- Channel: Ohio State
- Length: 31:56
- Views: 328
- Likes: 16
- Published: 2025-09-08T05:12:07-07:00
- Category: People & Blogs
- Language: en-US

## Description

What contributes to a strong graduate application to Ohio State?
This presentation is designed to share insights on:
Statements of purpose, including:
Definitions and key elements
Reflection and research
Formatting and style
Letters of recommendation, including:
Choosing references
Timeline of process

## Transcript

Hello and welcome to the Crafting a
Competitive Statement of Purpose and Letter of Reference Strategies webinar.
My name is Caroline and I'll be your
presenter today.
To start out our presentation,
we'll have a quick discussion of your application materials and what those
materials say about you.
Then we'll move on to statements of
purpose, including definitions and key elements,
how to reflect and do your research, and formatting and style issues.
Then we'll talk about letters of
recommendation, including the timeline of the process,
how to choose your references, and how to invite and manage your
references.
When you apply for graduate school,
you'll have to submit certain materials that share information about yourself
with your program.
Your application contains basic personal
information, and your transcripts let your program see what courses you've
taken and how you performed.
Some programs request GRE or GMAT scores,
which demonstrate your quantitative and verbal reasoning skills.
Additionally, many programs require a CV,
short for curriculum vitae, or resume, which shows your previous professional
and academic experiences.
Today we'll be focusing on two other
materials that are required for most programs,
statements of purpose and letters of recommendation.
These are important because they're an
opportunity to share any information that isn't covered in your other materials and
show why you are the best fit for your program.
Let's begin by talking about statements
of purpose.
A statement of purpose is a one to two
page statement that conveys your readiness for graduate study,
relevant experiences to your area of interest,
your academic and professional goals, how the program will help you achieve
these goals, and why you are a good fit for this
program.
This document may also be referred to as
a personal statement or statement of intent.
Some programs have specific prompts for
their statements of purpose.
This presentation is a high level
overview of these documents and won't address every specific program,
but the concepts we cover today will be relevant to almost all statements of
purpose.
There are three key elements to a
statement of purpose.
The first is "why".
What is your reason for pursuing this
specific program?
What academic and career experiences have
led you here and what are your goals?
The next is "what".
What specific subject do you want to study?
What are your research areas of interest?
And then finally, "how".
Who in your department might you work
with?
What labs or resources on campus might
you use?
To figure out your "why" and "what",
you should do some self-reflection and think about some of these questions.
You've probably already thought about
many of them while deciding to go to grad school and finding a program.
But if you're trying to figure out where
to start, you can try journaling based on these
questions and the ones on the next page.
So first of all,
especially if you're applying to a program with a heavy research component,
think about what is your research area of interest.
You might want to propose a research
topic, but note that this might not be what you
end up working on if you're admitted to your program.
Why are you interested in this area?
When you're answering these two questions,
the more specific the better.
Additionally,
think about the experiences that have prepared you for graduate school and for
work in your area of interest, and any challenges you have overcome.
If there are any gaps or discrepancies in
your academic record, such as a semester with low grades that
impacted your GPA, you may wish to explain these briefly in
a short section.
But since statements of purpose are short,
it's best not to spend too much time on them.
In addition to self-reflection,
to figure out your "what" and "how", you should do some research about your
program.
Again,
you've probably already thought about many of these questions while searching
for a program that's a good fit for you.
So, for example,
what career path will this program prepare you for,
and how is this relevant to your goals?
What courses does this program offer that
you're excited about?
What research is being done in this
department that you're interested in?
What faculty members might you want to
work with or have as an advisor?
What labs or resources on campus might
you use?
What internships or outside opportunities
does this program offer?
These won't all be relevant to every
single program, and you don't have to address all of them
in your statement of purpose, but you should know a couple answers and
hit a couple key points.
If you're applying to multiple graduate
programs at multiple schools, these answers will be different and you
should write a different statement of purpose for each school.
Specificity is really important.
The first place you should start when
doing research is your program's website.
This website will have information about
your program's curriculum and other resources like alumni networks, labs,
or studios.
They will also have a directory of
faculty and often list their research interests.
If you're applying to a program where you
will be doing research, or if you will be seeking funding in the
form of a graduate associateship, you may wish to reach out to the faculty
members that you would like to work with.
You can check those faculty members'
websites to find information about them and their CVs,
and you can often find their publications online.
If you identify a faculty member or two
that you want to work with, you can reach out to them to introduce
yourself and ask about the program.
When doing that,
here are some tips to keep in mind.
First, be selective.
Don't send an e-mail blast to everyone in
the department.
Choose one or two professors who you
think are the best fit.
Second, be concise.
It's good to introduce yourself and share
a bit about your background and why you are interested in the program,
but don't send a 10 paragraph e-mail or a huge list of questions.
Professors are very busy and get emails
from many prospective students.
Finally,
show that you've done your research.
Professors are more likely to respond and
to respond positively if you've shown that you have already
learned a bit about the program and their work and can prove that they're a good
fit for you.
It can be helpful to read one or two of
their recent papers.
Keep in mind that if you're applying for
a program with a more professional focus, you may not want to reach out to a
faculty member.
Finally,
you can also contact students in the program to get their opinions on
professors or labs.
Some programs post student contact
information on their sites, or you can contact the program to ask.
This is a sample e-mail to a professor.
It reads: Dear Doctor Lee,
My name is Jane Smith and I'm a prospective applicant to the Department
of Linguistics.
I recently graduated from State
University with a major in linguistics.
I'm currently teaching English in Taiwan
through the Fulbright Program.
I am writing to introduce myself and
express my interest in your work on multilingualism.
I would like to deepen my knowledge of
psycholinguistic methods, such as those used in your 2022 paper on
bilingual Cantonese speakers, and apply them to the case of Taiwan.
I am interested to know if you are taking
new students in Autumn 2026.
I would also be grateful for any
information or advice you might give me.
Thank you for your time.
I hope to have the opportunity to meet
you soon.
Sincerely, Jane Smith.
So, as a breakdown,
this prospective student introduces themselves and indicates their interest
in this program at this school.
Then they give a little background on
their experience and indicate their interest in this professor's work.
They mention wanting to learn the methods
of a specific paper that they read, showing that they've done their research
and that they're a good fit to work with this professor.
Notice that this e-mail is really short.
In fact,
the student could probably add one or two more sentences of specific details about
their interests and experiences, but when we wrote this sample e-mail,
they wouldn't fit on the slide.
Even with those extra sentences,
the e-mail would only take a few minutes to read.
It conveys all of the necessary
information without taking too much of the professor's time and demonstrates the
applicant's interest and commitment.
While we can never guarantee that a
professor will answer your e-mail, an e-mail like this is more likely to
receive a response than an e-mail that is generic or one that is extremely long.
Now that you've done some research and
brainstorming, it's time to write your statement of
purpose.
But how do you know exactly what to write?
Many programs will provide statement of
purpose prompts.
We're going to look at a few of those in
a moment.
If your program offers a prompt,
then that's easy, you should follow it.
But if your program doesn't offer a
prompt, we'll also talk about how to figure out
what to write.
We are going to look at two different
statement of purpose prompts.
The first is for the Master of City and
Regional Planning.
There are four required questions.
One,
why are you interested in studying city and regional planning?
Two,
why are you interested in pursuing this graduate degree at The Ohio State
University?
Three,
what sort of impact do you hope to have on the profession after graduating from
the Master of City and Regional Planning program at The Ohio State University?
And four,
what professional and personal experiences and values will you bring to
the Master of City and Regional Planning program and to the profession?
There are also three optional questions.
One, share any personal challenges,
barriers, or hardships you faced, how you overcame them,
and how they have shaped your academic experience and professional goals.
This could be an opportunity for you to
explain something on your transcript or resume that may raise concerns to the
admissions committee.
Two,
please share if there are particular life experiences or skill sets that would make
you an asset to the City and Regional Planning section of the Knowlton School.
That's our architecture school.
And three,
are there particular faculty at The Ohio State University that inspired you to
apply to the Master of City and Regional Planning program?
Are there particular research projects or
courses that they lead that you would like to join or enroll in?
The second statement of purpose prompt is
for the PhD in City and Regional Planning.
The PhD program calls their statement of
purpose a statement of research intent.
This is very similar to a statement of
purpose, but indicates a greater emphasis on
research.
The prompt is: The Statement of Research
Interest is a one to three page narrative that answers why the applicant wants to
pursue a doctorate in City and Regional Planning from The Ohio State University
and what the applicant wants to do once they earn that degree.
It is important to relate not only how
your personal and educational experiences thus far have led to your decision to
pursue this degree, but also details about the research
direction you wish to pursue.
A clear research direction is critical to
determine fit with the program and potential faculty advisors.
Let's compare these two prompts.
Both prompts ask why you want to study
this topic at Ohio State.
This is the basic question of a statement
of purpose.
They both ask about your previous
experiences, but the Master of City and Regional
Planning emphasizes professional experiences,
while the PhD emphasizes educational experiences.
When asking about what you want to go on
to do after Graduate School, the Master of City and Regional Planning
again has a stronger emphasis on the profession.
For the master's program,
it is optional to talk about faculty courses or research that you're
interested in, but for the PhD, it's required to talk about your research
interests.
So why are these two prompts different?
Let's compare the descriptions of the two
programs.
The Master of City and Regional Planning
description reads: The MCRP program will prepare you for a
leadership position through the integration of theory and practice.
We teach you the methods and skills
necessary to lead in the creation of sustainable and just cities and regions.
The PhD program description reads: Doctoral students join a select program
with a productive and dynamic faculty and participate in collaborative studies.
Our vibrant faculty conducts research in
vanguard areas of planning and develop connections to work locally and
internationally.
So the Master of City and Regional
Planning is preparing you for a leadership position in the field, while the PhD program has a much stronger
focus on doing research in the field.
The Master's program statement of purpose
should demonstrate more about you as a professional,
while the PhD statement of purpose should demonstrate more about you as a
researcher.
Not all programs have statement of
purpose prompts, so if yours doesn't, you should lean on the research you've
done about this program.
Notice how the statement of purpose
prompts we just talked about varied based on the purpose of the program.
So what is the purpose of your program?
Let that guide you and write while
keeping in mind the three key elements of why, what, and how.
Now we'll talk a little bit about
formatting.
When formatting your statement of purpose,
we recommend checking your program site for requirements.
In the absence of specific guidance from
your program, most statements of purpose are one to two
single spaced pages with 12 point font.
However, if you're unsure,
you can always contact your program to ask.
Here are some important points regarding
style.
First, be concise.
Don't be repetitive or include
unnecessary details.
Don't tell your whole life story either.
Focus on just what's recent and what's
relevant, and don't just repeat information that's
already visible in your CV or resume.
Statements of purpose are short,
so make sure to make the most of your space.
Second, speak the language of your field.
Your application will be reviewed by
people in the field, so you don't need to explain basic
concepts.
But at the same time,
the reviewers might not be in your specific subfield,
so it's best not to use too much extremely niche jargon.
Finally,
proofread to make sure there are no spelling or grammar errors.
You can get help from friends or family
members, or, if you're a college student, your school's writing center.
Professors or advisors at your current or
previous institution are also especially good resources for feedback because they
know what makes a good statement of purpose in their field.
If you're considering using AI,
Please note that you are responsible for developing your own ideas in your
statement of purpose, admissions essay, and CV.
If you're thinking about using AI to help,
you should ask the program you're applying to if this is permitted and if
it is what their policy is for proper use.
To close out our section on statements of
purpose, we're going to look at three sets of
examples that could come from real statements of purpose.
For each set,
think about which of the two is stronger.
First,
let's compare these two samples about why an applicant wants to study astronomy,
each of which could be part of a larger statement of purpose.
Sample number one reads: As an undergraduate,
I had the opportunity to work with an astronomy professor on a project that
used infrared observations to study the formation of stars.
I was fascinated by how much we could
learn about things so far away in space and time from here on Earth.
Sample number two reads: I have been captivated by space since I
was a young child.
I loved to read books about the different
planets of the solar system and visit the planetarium with my family.
So take a moment to pause and think about
which one is stronger.
The answer is the first one.
The second one shows a lot of passion for
the subject, but it relies on cliches and isn't very
specific.
The applicant might be really impressive,
but the things that they mentioned are true of lots of people.
On the other hand,
the first is more specific and demonstrates that the applicant has
experience in the field.
When they say that they like the subject,
they say why, and say so in a way that demonstrates
knowledge and experience while still showing a sense of wonder and a bit of
personality.
Now let's compare these two sentences
from a paragraph about why an applicant wants to come to Ohio State.
Number one: I would like to study
Electrical and Computer Engineering in your esteemed program because it is a
highly ranked program with excellent professors. Number two: At Ohio State, I hope to further develop my research on
photodetector technologies by working with Doctor Reano in the Integrated
Optics Laboratory.
Pause for a moment to think about which
you think is stronger.
The answer is the second sentence.
The first one is really vague.
You could write this about any school.
It's a nice thing to say, but the department already knows that
they have good professors.
The second one shows that the applicant
has done their research and is really specific.
This is a real lab and professor at Ohio State.
It demonstrates what the applicant's
interests are and that those interests are a good fit with the department.
It shows that the applicant has the potential to be someone who contributes
to the department by researching in a lab.
As a note,
many applicants ask us if it's OK to mention a professor by name if you
haven't emailed them or if you've emailed them and didn't receive a response.
It's important to be truthful in a
statement of purpose.
Don't pretend you spoke with a professor
if you didn't. However, if you know of a professor who you might
like to work with and haven't spoken to them yet,
you can still mention their name in your statement of purpose and say that you'd
like to work with them. Finally, let's compare these two samples from a
paragraph about the applicant's previous experiences. Number one: I majored in Environmental Studies and
after taking a class on environmental policy,
I landed an internship with a local environmental NGO before taking a job at
the Bureau of Land Management. Now, I'd like to advance my career by gaining
my Masters of Public administration.
Number two: While interning at the Clean Water
Organization, I helped advocate for legislation to
protect a local river.
Holding advocacy days at the Statehouse
made me realize how important it is to have people in the environmental field
with a strong background in public administration and policy.
Pause for a minute to think about which of these is stronger.
The answer is the second sample.
The first one isn't badly written,
but it doesn't have any information that you wouldn't see on the applicant's
transcript or CV.
Statements of purpose are short,
so you don't want to waste space repeating information.
The second one digs into a particular experience that the applicant had and how
it was important in their journey.
The applicant can provide further
information about their other experiences later on in the statement of purpose.
This concludes our discussion of statements of purpose.
Now we'll move on to talk about letters of recommendation.
Most of our programs require three letters of recommendation,
though some can require more or less.
Check your program's website for more
information about the number of required letters of recommendation.
This is a basic breakdown of our letter
of recommendation process.
You should start thinking about your
letters of recommendation early in the application process. Before you apply,
you should contact potential references.
Then,
once it's time for you to actually apply, you enter the details of those references,
including their contact information, in the application.
After you submit your application and it finishes processing in our system,
your references will receive an invitation from us to submit their
letters of recommendation online. Finally, any time before the deadline,
you can manage your references through your applicant center.
Because of the way our letter of recommendation process works,
it's really important that you not wait until the last moment to apply.
All required application materials must
be received by the application deadline, including your letters of recommendation.
Allow sufficient time for your application to post to our system and for
invitation emails to be sent to your recommenders.
During peak times and holidays, this can take up to six days.
Additionally, make sure to allow time for your
recommenders to write and submit their letters.
So who should write your letters of
recommendation? Well, first of all, check your program's website.
Most programs will request most or all references to be from academics familiar
with your work.
Some programs might also request letters
from supervisors, especially programs intended for working
professionals.
Your letter of recommendation writers
should be people who know you well.
It's best to have people you've worked
with closely: not a professor who had you for one big class,
but a professor you've done research with, TAed for,
or taken multiple small classes with.
Finally,
your letters of recommendation should never come from friends or family.
So what makes a good letter of recommendation?
A good letter of recommendation should
demonstrate your ability to be successful in a graduate program.
It will provide the outside perspective of someone who has worked with you,
and it should include specific examples.
Letters of recommendation can be an
opportunity to demonstrate your soft skills, such as teamwork, problem solving,
creativity, self-discipline, resilience, autonomy, and interpersonal skills.
Obviously,
you can't control what your letter writers say,
so you should choose someone who thinks highly of you and can share concrete
information.
Here are some tips for contacting your
references. First of all, stay connected with them.
It will be easier to get a letter of recommendation if the person remembers
you and knows what you're up to.
Second of all, reach out in advance.
Keep in mind that professors are busy and
may not be able to write a letter of recommendation with short notice. Finally,
you should send your CV or resume and statement of purpose and talk about your
goals.
Make sure that your professor knows about
your application so that they can recommend you as specifically as possible.
Here's an example e-mail from a student to a former professor of theirs asking
for a letter of recommendation.
It reads: Dear Professor Singh,
I hope you have been doing well.
I'm writing to ask if you would be
willing to write a letter of recommendation for my application to the
PhD in History at Ohio State.
Since I graduated,
I've been working at the City Archives.
I'm excited about this program because...
I've attached my CV and a draft of my
statement of purpose.
The application is due on December 1st.
I am planning to submit around a month from now in, early November,
so you would receive an e-mail invitation with a link to submit the letter shortly
thereafter. Best regards, Ash.
So notice how this student gives a little
information to their professor about what they've been up to and why they're
interested in this particular program.
We've had to cut some details out for
space.
Then they attach their CV and a draft of
their statement of purpose.
This information enables the professor to
write the best and most specific recommendation possible. Lastly,
they give a little information about the application timeline.
They're writing this e-mail two months
before the application is due, so the professor has plenty of time to
write and submit their letter of recommendation. All right,
now that you've contacted your references, it's time to apply to Ohio State.
To invite a reference, first start your application. Then,
enter your recommenders' information under the Final Steps/Submit page.
Finally, when everything's filled out,
submit your application.
After your application finishes
processing, your recommender will receive an e-mail
from us. This can take up to six days, depending on when your application is
submitted.
That's another great reason to submit
early.
When you input your references'
information, you can also choose whether to waive
access to your letters of recommendation.
If you select "yes" to waive access,
you will not be able to view your letters of recommendation. If you select "no",
you can request permission from your program via e-mail to view them.
If you do not want to waive, it's best to get permission from your
references, as some references may wish to submit
confidentially.
After your application has finished
processing, you will get an Ohio State username.
You can use that username to go to go.osu.
edu/lor to add, edit or remind a recommender.
On this site, you can also change your response to the
wave access question, but only if the recommendation status is
"not started". As a reminder, "yes" means that you will not have access
to view your letters and "no" means that you will be able to request
access from your program.
That brings us to the end of our
presentation. As some final reminders, for statements of purpose,
do your research, be specific and proofread.
For letters of recommendation, ask and apply in advance,
choose references who know you well, and manage your references at go.osu.
edu/lor. Thank you for watching.
If you have any questions that weren't
covered in this presentation or questions about other elements of the application
process, you can reach out to us at
gpadmissions@osu.edu or 614-292-9444.
You can also go to our website,
gpadmissions.osu.edu, to find more information about the
application process, a link to schedule a virtual or in-person
meeting with our counselors, and a link to our other recorded
presentations.

results.title: Differences in Degree Programs
results.author: Cindy Grey
results.text:
Differences in Degree Programs

| Program | Length | Structure | Funding | End Result |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Prof. Masters | 1 Year | Coursework with a team project | Mostly funded by the student | Student will be prepared to work in industry |
| MA / MS | 2 Years | About 1/2 coursework and 1/2 research project | Some funding may be available, but mostly funded by the student | Student will be prepared to work in industry and or in a research environment (not their own research project) |
| PhD | 4+ | Very little coursework - intensive degree | Fully funded (department pays research tuition, stipend and health insurance) | Student will be prepared to work in industry, in a research environment and on their own research projects |

## You are here
* [**Home](https://ed.stanford.edu/)
